The implementation of the Planning Portal system, in its current form, is proving to be highly inefficient and counterproductive to the wider objectives of the construction sector. While digitalisation was intended to streamline the planning process, the reality on the ground is quite the opposite. Delays, inconsistent requirements between local authorities, poor system usability, and a lack of clear accountability are creating significant bottlenecks at every stage of the application process.
For developers, contractors, and consultants alike, the system introduces unnecessary friction. Simple applications are taking far longer than they should, communication channels are fragmented, and the lack of standardisation across councils undermines any notion of a unified national framework. This inefficiency is not only frustrating for industry professionals but is also directly impacting project timelines, costs, and overall delivery capacity.
Given the Government’s stated ambition to significantly increase housing supply, it is difficult to see how these targets can realistically be achieved under the current conditions. The planning stage is a critical gateway in the development pipeline, and when this gateway is slow, inconsistent, and poorly managed, it restricts the entire system downstream.
Without urgent reform, focusing on simplification, standardisation, improved system performance, and greater accountability, the Planning Portal risks remaining a major obstacle rather than an enabler of development. As it stands, the level of inefficiency within the system reflects a broader lack of operational effectiveness within the planning process, which ultimately undermines confidence in the sector’s ability to deliver at the scale required.
